text: A man is looking at a portrait and says: "Brothers and sisters I have none, but this man's father is my father's son." Who is the man in the portrait?
explanation:
"My father's son" — since the speaker has no brothers, this must be the speaker himself.
So: "This man's father is ME."
Therefore the man in the portrait is his son.

text: There are 3 doors: behind one is a car, behind two are goats. You pick door 1. The host opens door 3 (showing a goat) and offers you to switch to door 2. Should you switch?
explanation:
This is the Monty Hall problem.

Initial pick: 1/3 chance of car, 2/3 chance of goat.
The host always reveals a goat from the remaining doors.

If you stay: you win only if your initial pick was right (1/3).
If you switch: you win if your initial pick was wrong (2/3).

Always switch! It doubles your probability from 1/3 to 2/3.

Intuition: Imagine 100 doors. You pick 1. Host opens 98 goat doors. Would you switch to the remaining one? Of course — 99/100 chance!

text: Four people need to cross a bridge at night with one flashlight. The bridge holds 2 people max. Crossing times: A=1min, B=2min, C=5min, D=10min. A pair crosses at the slower person's speed. What is the minimum total crossing time?
explanation:
Optimal strategy:
1. A and B cross → 2 min (total: 2)
2. A returns → 1 min (total: 3)
3. C and D cross → 10 min (total: 13)
4. B returns → 2 min (total: 15)
5. A and B cross → 2 min (total: 17)

Key insight: Send the two slowest together (step 3) so you only pay the 10-min penalty once, not twice!

Naive strategy (A escorts everyone): 2+1+5+1+10 = 19 min — worse!

text: If all Bloops are Razzies, and all Razzies are Lazzies, are all Bloops definitely Lazzies?
explanation:
Transitive property: Bloops⊂Razzies⊂Lazzies.
All Bloops are Razzies, and all Razzies are Lazzies, so all Bloops are Lazzies. This is basic syllogistic logic.

text: A farmer has 17 sheep. All but 9 die. How many sheep are left?
explanation:
"All but 9 die" = 9 survive. Answer: 9 sheep.
Common mistake: computing 17−9=8.

text: How many times can you subtract 5 from 25?
explanation:
You can subtract 5 from 25 only ONCE. After that, you're subtracting 5 from 20, not from 25!
25−5=20 (done once from 25). The next time it's 20−5, not 25−5.

text: I have two coins that total 30 cents. One is not a nickel. What are the two coins?
explanation:
"One is not a nickel" — but the OTHER one can be! A quarter (25¢) + a nickel (5¢) = 30¢.
The quarter is "not a nickel." The statement is true — it just doesn't say NEITHER is a nickel.

text: Three light switches outside a room control three bulbs inside. You can flip switches as much as you want but can only enter the room once. How do you determine which switch controls which bulb?
choices: Flip all on then off one by one
Turn switch 1 ON for 10 min, then OFF; switch 2 ON; enter
explanation:
Turn switch 1 ON for 10 minutes, then turn it OFF.
Turn switch 2 ON. Enter the room.
• Hot bulb (off) → switch 1 (was on long enough to heat up)
• Lit bulb → switch 2 (currently on)
• Cold, off bulb → switch 3
Uses the property of heat, not just light!

text: You have a 3-gallon jug and a 5-gallon jug. How do you measure exactly 4 gallons?
choices: Fill 5, pour into 3, empty 3, pour remaining 2 into 3, fill 5, pour into 3 until full
Fill 3, pour into 5, fill 3, pour into 5
explanation:
Step 1: Fill 5-gal jug. Pour into 3-gal jug. (5-gal has 2, 3-gal has 3)
Step 2: Empty 3-gal jug. Pour 2 gallons from 5-gal into 3-gal. (5-gal has 0, 3-gal has 2)
Step 3: Fill 5-gal jug. Pour into 3-gal jug until full (needs 1 gallon). (5-gal has 4, 3-gal has 3)
Result: 5-gallon jug has exactly 4 gallons!

text: Two fathers and two sons go fishing. They catch exactly 3 fish, each person catches one. How?
choices: There are only 3 people (grandfather, father, son)
explanation:
There are only 3 people: a grandfather, his son (who is also a father), and his grandson.
The middle person is both a son AND a father.
2 fathers (grandfather + father) and 2 sons (father + grandson) = 3 people, 3 fish.

text: 100 people in a line, each wearing a red or blue hat. Each can see all hats in front but not their own. From back to front, each must guess their hat color. They can agree on a strategy beforehand. How many can be guaranteed to guess correctly?
explanation:
Strategy: Person 100 (back) counts the red hats they see.
• If even → says "red". If odd → says "blue".
Person 99 sees all hats in front, heard person 100, and can deduce their own color.
Person 98 heard 99 and 100, deduces theirs, and so on.

Result: Person 100 has a 50/50 chance (sacrifice). Persons 1-99 are guaranteed correct = 99 saved.

text: A king tells 100 prisoners: tomorrow I'll line you up and put a number (1-100, may repeat) on each forehead. Each sees all numbers except their own. Each writes a guess for their own number. If at least ONE guesses correctly, all go free. Strategy?
choices: Each guesses the number they see most
Assign each person an offset — person k guesses (S mod 100 – offset)
explanation:
Assign prisoner k the "responsibility" for the case where the total sum mod 100 = k.
Prisoner k calculates the sum of all numbers they see, call it S_others.
Prisoner k guesses: (k − S_others) mod 100.

If the actual total S mod 100 = k, then prisoner k's guess = S − S_others = their own number.
Exactly one value of k from 0-99 equals S mod 100, so exactly one prisoner always guesses right!

text: You're on an island with two tribes: one always tells truth, one always lies. You meet a person at a fork. One road leads to safety. What ONE question guarantees the safe road?
choices: Which road do you live on?
If I asked the other tribe, which road would they say is safe?
explanation:
"If I asked the other tribe which road is safe, what would they say?"
Then take the OPPOSITE road.

• Truth-teller: honestly reports what liar would say (wrong road) → go opposite.
• Liar: lies about what truth-teller would say (also wrong road) → go opposite.

Both give you the wrong road, so you always go the other way!

text: 5 pirates divide 100 gold coins. The most senior proposes a split; all vote. If ≥50% approve, it's accepted. Otherwise, that pirate is thrown overboard and the next proposes. What does the most senior pirate propose?
choices: 98 for himself, 1 each to two others
explanation:
Work backwards:
2 pirates: Senior takes 100, votes for self (50% = pass).
3 pirates: #3 gives 1 coin to #1 (who'd get 0 with 2 pirates). Split: 99,0,1.
4 pirates: #4 gives 1 to #2. Split: 99,0,1,0.
5 pirates: #5 needs 3 votes (including self). Gives 1 coin each to pirates who'd get 0 in 4-pirate scenario (#2 and #4). Split: 98,0,1,0,1.

text: A clock loses 10 minutes every hour. It was set correctly at noon. What time does it show when the actual time is 6 PM?
explanation:
In 6 actual hours, the clock runs 6×50/60 = 5 hours (50 min per actual hour).
Shows: 12:00 + 5 hours = 5:00 PM.
It lost 10 min/hr × 6 hrs = 60 minutes total.

text: What is the angle between the hour and minute hands at 3:15?
explanation:
At 3:15:
Minute hand: at 3 (90° from 12).
Hour hand: at 3 + 15/60 of the way to 4 = 3.25. Angle = 3.25×30° = 97.5°.
Difference: 97.5°−90° = 7.5°.

Common mistake: thinking it's 0° because both point near 3.

text: A snail climbs 3 feet up a 30-foot wall each day, but slides back 2 feet each night. How many days to reach the top?
explanation:
Net progress: 1 ft/day. But on the last day, it reaches the top and stops.
After 27 nights: 27 feet. Day 28: climbs 3 → reaches 30 feet. Done!
28 days.

Common mistake: 30/1=30 days (ignores the final day's climb without sliding back).

text: You have 25 horses and can race 5 at a time (no stopwatch). Minimum races to find the top 3 fastest?
explanation:
Race 1-5: Race all 25 in groups of 5 (5 races). Know winner of each group.
Race 6: Race the 5 group winners. Now you know the overall fastest horse.
Race 7: The 2nd and 3rd place could be:
• 2nd and 3rd from race 6
• 2nd from the fastest horse's original group
• 2nd from the race-6-2nd-place horse's group
Race these 5 candidates → top 2 of this race are overall 2nd and 3rd.

Total: 7 races.
